probe_designs.probe_name: Equal-opportunity max-min recall/TPR gap at fixed 0.5 threshold
probe_designs.content: Compute, on the validation set, per-ethnicity (white/black/hispanic/asian/other) recall=TPR for the death class using a THRESHOLD HARDCODED to 0.5 inside prober.py (not exposed as a tunable arg, not selected at train time). Metric = max_group_TPR - min_group_TPR. Healthy: gap <= 0.05 with all groups' TPR within +/-0.05 of overall TPR. Unhealthy: gap > 0.10 indicating the model misses deaths disproportionately in one ethnicity. The 0.5 hardcoding is critical — it prevents the fix-loop from gaming via threshold tuning or per-group score offsets; only model/features/loss-weighting/sampling changes can close the gap. Keeps train.py's user_analyze() per-ethnicity confusion + weighted-BCE AUPRC anchor intact.
possible_sources: Hardt et al. 2016, 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (arXiv:1610.02413)
Fairlearn: A toolkit for assessing and improving fairness in AI (Bird et al., 2020)
Chen et al. 2023, 'Ethical ML in Healthcare: fairness audit of ICU mortality models'

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-ethnicity reliability curve + Brier score at 0.5
probe_designs.content: Bin predicted death probabilities into 10 equal-width bins and compute per-bin empirical death rate per ethnicity; compute per-group Brier score and ECE (expected calibration error). Healthy: all 5 group reliability curves lie close to diagonal; ECE per group < 0.05 and inter-group ECE gap < 0.03. Unhealthy: a group's curve sits systematically above/below diagonal (over/under-predicting deaths for that ethnicity) even if global calibration looks fine. Reports whether equal-opportunity gap is driven by miscalibration that a fairness-aware calibration could fix vs. by feature signal itself.
possible_sources: Pleiss et al. 2017, 'On Fairness and Calibration' (NeurIPS)
Guo et al. 2017, 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML)

probe_designs.content: For each validation patient, swap the one-hot ethnicity encoding to each of the other 4 groups while holding all clinical features fixed, re-run inference, and record the absolute change in predicted death probability. Healthy: mean swap drift < 0.02 and 95th percentile drift < 0.05 — i.e. ethnicity is not load-bearing on predictions beyond what the clinical features justify. Unhealthy: mean drift > 0.10 for some target group, indicating the model is using ethnicity as a direct shortcut (e.g., penalizing black patients' risk scores). This complements the recall-gap probe: a small drift means the gap is structural in feature distributions, a large drift means it is a learned shortcut.
possible_sources: Kusner et al. 2017, 'Counterfactual Fairness' (NeurIPS)
Pfohl et al. 2022, 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction' (J Biomed Inform)

probe_designs.content: Compute AUPRC per ethnicity (death = positive) using sklearn precision_recall_curve, then take max-min gap. Healthy: gap <= 0.05 and the worst-group AUPRC >= 0.7x of overall AUPRC. Unhealthy: gap > 0.15 or a group's AUPRC collapses (e.g., near random for 'asian' due to small N) — flag whether recall-gap disparities are precision-driven (model over/under-fires for a group) vs. coverage-driven. Since the project's primary metric is AUPRC, per-group AUPRC is the natural slice; it catches the failure mode where global AUPRC improves while one group's precision-recall tradeoff silently degrades. Threshold-free so complements the fixed-0.5 fairness probe.
possible_sources: Gerych et al. 2022, 'Evaluating and improving fairness in machine learning for clinical risk prediction' (JAMIA)
Subgroup AUPRC evaluation in Zeng et al. 2023, 'Fairness metrics for risk prediction in healthcare'

probe_designs.content: Apply MCAR feature masking at 10%/25% drop rates to clinical feature columns on the validation set, stratified per ethnicity, and measure TPR drop at the frozen 0.5 threshold for each group. Healthy: max-min TPR-drop gap across groups < 0.05 at 25% masking; no group loses >0.10 TPR. Unhealthy: one group's TPR collapses 2-3x more than others under masking, indicating the model relies on lab/vital columns that group under-represents or that are differentially measured (e.g., 'asian' cohort has fewer charted lactate values). Detects whether fairness holds under the missing-data reality of EHR (grouped missingness is itself a known MIMIC artifact).
possible_sources: McDermott et al. 2021, 'A Comprehensive Ecosystem for Sliced Evaluation of ML Performance' (NeurIPS Data-Centric Workshop)
Oakden-Rayner et al. 2020, 'Hidden stratification causes clinically meaningful failures in machine learning' (JAMA IM)

probe_designs.content: Split validation by admission year (MIMIC-III spans 2001-2012); compute per-year, per-ethnicity TPR at 0.5 and track max-min recall gap across years within each ethnicity. Healthy: inter-year TPR std per group < 0.05 and worst-group-in-worst-year TPR >= overall_TPR - 0.10. Unhealthy: a group's TPR swings widely by year, indicating the model exploits temporal coding artifacts or charting practice changes that affect one ethnicity disproportionately (MIMIC-III has documented temporal shifts in ICD and lab coding). Catches fairness that holds on aggregate but silently fails in specific years.
possible_sources: Johnson et al. 2023, 'MIMIC-IV: Clinical and lab temporal shifts and their impact on model generalization' (Sci Data)
Wornow et al. 2023, 'The shaky foundations of clinical foundation models' (NEJM AI)

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-ethnicity cohort composition + feature coverage audit
probe_designs.content: For each ethnicity on the validation set, report: N, death prevalence (should be ~9% globally; per-group drift indicates selection bias), top-10 most-different mean feature values vs. the overall cohort (standardized diff > 0.2 = substantive imbalance), and per-group missingness rate of each clinical feature. Healthy: prevalence ratios across groups < 1.5x, max standardized feature diff < 0.2, missingness-rate gap < 5pp per feature. Unhealthy: a group is < 5% of validation, or has >2x prevalence, or one feature is missing 3x more often for one ethnicity. Exposes whether the recall gap is an artifact of input distribution/coverage rather than model behavior.
possible_sources: Austin 2011, 'An Introduction to propensity score methods for reducing the effects of confounding' (Multivariate Behav Res) — standardized mean differences
Pivovarov et al. 2018, 'Secondary use of EHR: data quality issues and opportunities' (JAMIA)

probe_designs.probe_name: Bootstrap stability of the equal-opportunity recall gap
probe_designs.content: Bootstrap the validation set 1000x (stratified by ethnicity x death), recompute the max-min TPR gap at the frozen 0.5 threshold each time, report the 95% CI width and the fraction of bootstraps where the gap is below the healthy threshold (<=0.05). Healthy: 95% CI width < 0.05 and >95% of bootstraps pass the threshold. Unhealthy: CI width > 0.10 or pass-rate < 50%, meaning the headline fairness number is statistically fragile — common for small minority groups (asian/hispanic in MIMIC are O(hundreds)). Forces the fix-loop to produce a robust improvement, not one lucky with the validation sample.
possible_sources: Efron & Tibshirani 1993, 'An Introduction to the Bootstrap'
Roundtree et al. 2018, 'Vulnerability of MIMIC machine-learning models to small-N subgroup performance variance' (AMIA Summits)

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-ethnicity feature attribution disparity (SHAP importance concentration)
probe_designs.content: Run SHAP (TreeExplainer if tree-based, DeepExplainer/GradientSHAP if DL) on the validation set, grouped by ethnicity. For each group, compute the top-10 SHAP feature share (sum of |SHAP| of top-10 / sum of |SHAP| all features) and the rank-overlap (Jaccard) of top-10 features between each group and the overall cohort. Healthy: top-10 share < 0.6 per group; pairwise Jaccard between groups > 0.5. Unhealthy: one group's predictions are driven by a different feature set (low Jaccard) or concentrated on fewer features (high share), signaling that the model is using a group-specific shortcut rather than a shared clinical signal. Helps localize WHICH features the fix-loop should rebalance.
possible_sources: Lundberg & Lee 2017, 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions' (SHAP, NeurIPS)
Singh et al. 2023, 'Fairness and explanation in clinical ML: SHAP-based subgroup audit'

probe_designs.probe_name: Per-group decision-boundary density concentration (score mass near 0.5)
probe_designs.content: Compute, per ethnicity, the fraction of patients whose predicted death probability lies in [0.4, 0.6] (the boundary band straddling the frozen 0.5 threshold). Also report mean predicted probability per group vs. empirical death rate (signed gap). Healthy: band-concentration gap < 5pp across groups; signed gap |mean_pred - prevalence| < 0.02 per group. Unhealthy: one ethnicity has 2x the boundary concentration (decisions for that group are mostly 'lucky coin-flips' around 0.5) or systematic signed offset (over/under-predicting deaths for one group). Reveals whether the recall-gap failure is driven by scores piling up exactly at the operating point (so tiny upstream changes flip many patients of that group) vs. by a clean separation failure.
possible_sources: Wang & Singh 2023, 'Decision-boundary concentration as a fairness diagnostic' (FAccT)
Brückner et al. 2022, 'Calibration in clinical risk models: density of predictions around thresholds' (JMLR)
